pattern: Only two values in the whole array can ever be its minimum or its maximum, so among any three entries at least one is neither - the answer never requires looking past the first few elements.
hints: Think about how many array entries could possibly tie for being the single overall minimum or the single overall maximum at the same time.
Compute the array's minimum and maximum once, then look for an entry equal to neither of them among just the first few positions instead of the whole array.

With three or more elements, at least one of the first three can't simultaneously equal both the global minimum and the global maximum, so scan only the first three entries and return whichever one isn't equal to either; with fewer than three elements every entry must be the minimum or the maximum, so return -1.

edge_inputs.description: a single element is always both the minimum and the maximum
two elements are always exactly the minimum and the maximum
three elements with one clear middle value
exactly three elements already in ascending order
exactly three elements already in descending order
several valid middle candidates exist among five elements
the maximum then the minimum appear before the only valid answer
the global minimum and maximum sit at both ends of a longer run

stress.description: near-maximum length drawn without replacement from the full value range
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100))
    nums = rng.sample(range(1, 101), n)
    return [nums]

stress.description: lengths clustered right around the 3-element threshold
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(1, 4)
    nums = rng.sample(range(1, 101), n)
    return [nums]
